ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fish tikka; closest parent 'fish food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific tikka/marinated grilled fish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically covers fish tikka; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Machhli Tikka is approximately 150 g, consisting of two to three pieces of marinated grilled dory fish.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, Rohu, cooked / Grilled fish with spice marinade — adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central (Pangasius, cooked)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Machhli Tikka uses dory fish (pangasius) as the base. USDA FoodData Central entry for pangasius/swai (cooked) provides the fish base macros. Marinade additions (yogurt, lemon, spices, oil) are estimated from ICMR-NIN data for typical tikka marinades. Values represent a composite estimate for the finished grilled product per 100 g edible portion. Energy calculated from Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant fish and meat dishes — analogous estimate based on grilled fish with spice marinade
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Machhli Tikka or fish tikka. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for protein-rich fish dishes in the range of 0–40 due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content. A GI of 30 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate for a marinated grilled fish dish with minimal carbohydrate from marinade spices and yogurt. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 4.2) / 100 ≈ 1.3, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 2.8 × 1.5 = 4.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices and salt
hypertension.guidance: Machhli Tikka contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (465 mg per standard 150 g serving), which represents about 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Machhli Tikka has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of only 1.3 per serving, making it a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though portion control and avoidance of high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with moderate unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dory fish provides a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (approximately 4:1) and is a source of omega-3 fatty acids; the grilled preparation without heavy sauces supports a heart-healthy dietary pattern, though sodium content should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Machhli Tikka (also spelled Mahcli Tikka or Machi Tikka) is an Indian grilled fish dish in which pieces of fish — commonly dory (pangasius) in Singapore restaurant contexts — are marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture containing lemon juice, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garam masala, ginger, and garlic, then cooked over high heat in a tandoor oven or on a grill. The result is a lightly charred, aromatic fish with a tender, flaky interior and a mildly spiced, tangy exterior. The dish is a seafood adaptation of the classic Indian tikka preparation tradition, which is widely served across North Indian and Mughlai restaurants. In Singapore, it appears on menus at Indian restaurants such as Dumpukht Grills & Curry, where it is described as dory fish marinated in a hint of lemon. Nutritionally, Machhli Tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with approximately 18.5 g protein and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it suitable for low-glycemic and high-protein dietary patterns. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 30), and the glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 1.3. The fat content is moderate at 6.2 g per 100 g, with a favourable unsaturated fat profile from the fish. Sodium is a consideration at approximately 310 mg per 100 g due to the spice marinade. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compatible. It is clinically suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease, with moderate sodium awareness recommended for those with hypertension.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish tikka?
What fish is used in Machhli Tikka?
Is fish tikka good for diabetics?
What is the GI of fish tikka?
What spices are in fish tikka marinade?

confidence_reason: The dish is identified as dory fish tikka from a Singapore Indian restaurant. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (pangasius, cooked) and ICMR-NIN data for tikka-style preparations, as no exact entry for Machhli Tikka or dory fish tikka exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for protein-dominant fish dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base fish (pangasius/dory) has well-characterised USDA data and the tikka marinade contribution is well-bounded.
notes: 1. The food name 'Mahcli Tikka' in the input is a phonetic/transliteration variant of the Hindi/Urdu word 'machhli' (मछली) meaning fish; the canonical English name is rendered as 'Machhli Tikka' with 'Fish Tikka' as the primary English alias. 2. The restaurant description states 'dory fish marinated in a hint of lemon'; dory in Singapore restaurant contexts refers to pangasius (Pangasianodon hypophthalmus), also known as swai or basa. 3. Nutrient values are composite estimates: fish base from USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 15264, Catfish/Pangasius cooked) adjusted for marinade additions per ICMR-NIN tikka preparation data. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this fish-based preparation with no hydrogenated fats in the marinade; confirmed zero per USDA source for pangasius. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for fish tikka was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central pangasius data and are not -1 as the source provides these values. 7. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient values are estimated from approved sources and documented accordingly.
